questions.q: In Tomasulo's algorithm, what is the primary purpose of the reservation stations?
options: To provide temporary storage for instructions waiting for their operands to become available
To replace the register file entirely in modern processors
To accelerate the fetch stage of the pipeline
To store branch prediction information for speculative execution
questions.explanation: Reservation stations decouple instruction issue from operand availability. Instructions wait in them until their required operands are ready from the common data bus. They enable out-of-order execution by removing dependencies on specific pipeline timing. Tomasulo's algorithm doesn't eliminate the register file; it augments it with reservation stations.

questions.q: Register renaming in out-of-order processors serves which primary function?
options: To increase the number of physical registers available to programs
To eliminate false dependencies by mapping architectural registers to different physical registers
To reduce power consumption in the register file
To simplify the instruction decoder logic
questions.explanation: Register renaming breaks false (anti and output) dependencies by assigning different physical registers to writes of the same architectural register. This allows truly independent instructions to execute out-of-order. Physical register count is hardware-dependent; renaming doesn't create more, but it enables better utilization.

questions.q: What distinguishes the reorder buffer (ROB) from the reservation stations in an out-of-order processor?
options: The ROB tracks instruction completion order for in-order retirement while reservation stations manage operand dependencies
The ROB is used only for memory operations while reservation stations handle all instructions
The reorder buffer eliminates branch prediction errors automatically
Reservation stations guarantee in-order execution; the ROB executes out-of-order
questions.explanation: The ROB enforces in-order commitment and precise exceptions by retiring instructions in program order, even though they executed out-of-order. Reservation stations track operand availability and issue ready instructions. They work together: instructions execute via reservation stations but retire via the ROB.

questions.q: A processor uses Tomasulo's algorithm with 4 reservation stations for integer operations. If all stations are full and a new integer instruction arrives, what happens?
options: The processor stalls instruction issue until a station becomes free
The new instruction is discarded and must be refetched
A reservation station is bypassed to allow immediate execution
The instruction buffer automatically expands
questions.explanation: When all reservation stations are full, the processor cannot issue further integer instructions until one completes execution and its station becomes available. This is an instruction-level structural hazard. Modern processors handle this with larger instruction queues to minimize stalls.

questions.q: In an out-of-order processor, an instruction's result is broadcast on the common data bus (CDB). Which hardware units listen to this broadcast?
options: Only the register file, which stores the final result
Reservation stations whose waiting instructions depend on this result, and the reorder buffer
Only the reorder buffer, which coordinates all result forwarding
Only the next instruction in program order
questions.explanation: The CDB broadcast allows dependent instructions in reservation stations to proceed once their operands arrive. The reorder buffer also monitors results to track completion. This broadcast mechanism is central to Tomasulo's algorithm for both dependency resolution and instruction tracking.

questions.q: What problem does register renaming solve that reservations stations alone cannot address?
options: Resolving data hazards from memory operations
Eliminating false dependencies that prevent out-of-order execution
Increasing the processor clock frequency
Reducing branch misprediction penalties
questions.explanation: Reservation stations handle true data dependencies by waiting for operands. Register renaming eliminates false dependencies (anti-dependencies and output dependencies) by giving each write a unique physical register. Without renaming, an instruction writing to r1 would block unrelated instructions reading r1 due to false dependencies.

questions.q: In a processor with register renaming, how many physical registers are typically needed compared to the 32 architectural registers in a 32-bit ISA?
options: Exactly 32, same as architectural registers
Typically 80-200+, to support multiple outstanding instructions and rename tables
More than 1000, one per possible instruction combination
The same number, but different internal representation
questions.explanation: Modern out-of-order processors use 80-200+ physical registers to accommodate multiple in-flight instructions, rename tables for recovery, and free list management. This surplus enables the processor to sustain high issue rates without waiting for architectural registers to be freed.

questions.q: When a branch misprediction is detected in an out-of-order processor, the reorder buffer must recover. Which step occurs first?
options: Discard all instructions after the mispredicted branch in the reorder buffer
Restore all physical registers to their checkpoint state
Restart fetch from the correct branch target
Flush the entire reservation stations and caches
questions.explanation: The ROB flushes mispredicted instructions first, preventing them from retiring and corrupting architectural state. Then fetch restarts from the correct address. Register restoration and reservation station clearing follow as part of recovery, but draining the ROB of wrong-path instructions is the immediate critical step.

questions.q: What is the role of the free list in a register-renaming system?
options: It tracks which physical registers have been renamed but not yet written
It maintains a list of available (free) physical registers that can be allocated to new instructions
It stores branch target addresses for speculative execution
It records program order for instruction retirement
questions.explanation: The free list tracks unallocated physical registers ready for assignment. When an instruction is decoded, a free physical register is assigned to its destination. When instructions retire, their old physical registers return to the free list. This management enables efficient use of the larger physical register file.

questions.q: A deeply out-of-order processor with 200 instructions in flight can tolerate a 150-cycle memory latency without stalling as often as a shallow pipeline. Why?
options: The deeper pipeline has more physical registers to hide latency
More independent instructions can be found in the window of execution to fill the latency gap
The memory system is slower in deeply out-of-order designs
Register renaming automatically prefetches memory values
questions.explanation: A large out-of-order execution window (200 in-flight instructions) can extract enough independent work to keep the pipeline busy during long memory latencies. A shallow pipeline or smaller window would stall waiting for the load result. This is a key advantage of wide, deep out-of-order designs.
